title: Can Churches Renew the 'Pursuit of Happiness'?
gist: Brad Wilcox and James Lynch argue America's 'happiness crash' is driven by the decline of marriage and family, and that churches can reverse it by building Peter Berger-style 'plausibility structures' through marriage and family ministries — citing strong outcome data from the nonprofit Communio's church partnerships.

insights.claim: University of Chicago economist Sam Peltzman's 2023 study found marriage is the single greatest differentiator between happy and unhappy Americans, and its retreat statistically explains most of the recent decline in overall happiness.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, James Lynch
insights.so_what: Pins the U.S. well-being decline to a single addressable variable (marriage rate), reframing 'happiness policy' around family formation.
insights.quote: marriage is the single greatest differentiator between happy and unhappy Americans
insights.locator: We are in the midst of a “happiness crash,”

insights.claim: Among religious Americans aged 25-55, married men and women are more than twice as likely to report being 'very happy' in life than their unmarried religious counterparts, per the General Social Survey.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, James Lynch
insights.so_what: Shows religion alone is insufficient — marriage adds a distinct happiness premium even within the faithful, sharpening the intervention target.
insights.quote: religious, married women and men aged 25 to 55 are more than twice as likely to report being “very happy” in life
insights.locator: According to the General Social Survey

insights.claim: Reviving marriage requires rebuilding what sociologist Peter Berger called 'plausibility structures' — institutions and communities that make a way of life feel livable and real — because belief systems cannot be revived through intellectual persuasion alone.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, James Lynch
insights.so_what: A reusable mental model: cultural change follows social structures, implying institution-building (not messaging) is the lever for family revival.
insights.quote: You have to rebuild the institutions and communities that make it feel livable and real.
insights.locator: This is Berger’s most important practical insight

insights.claim: Wives who attend church regularly with their husbands are 112% more likely to report being very happy in marriage, and husbands who worship alongside their wives are 212% more likely, per a 2025 IFS/Wheatley Institute report.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, James Lynch
insights.so_what: Quantifies joint religious practice as a powerful marital-satisfaction multiplier, especially for men.
insights.quote: husbands who worship regularly alongside their wives are 212% more likely to be very happy in marriage

insights.claim: Couples who attend church together are 30 to 50% less likely to divorce, per Wilcox's book 'Get Married.'
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, James Lynch
insights.so_what: Joint churchgoing materially lowers divorce risk — a concrete retention lever for the marriage-strengthening case.
insights.quote: Couples who attend church together are also 30 to 50% less likely to divorce
insights.locator: Churchgoing doesn’t just increase your odds

insights.claim: Communio, a nonprofit partnering with hundreds of churches, saw 22,149 people complete marriage/relationship ministry activities last year, with 74% of self-reported high-divorce-risk participants reporting very-satisfied-or-better marriages afterward and 57% of singles gaining relationship skills.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, James Lynch
insights.so_what: Provides a scalable, measurable ministry model with self-reported outcome metrics — a template for institutional family-formation investment.
insights.quote: 74% of those who self-reported being at high risk for divorce said they were very satisfied or better with their marriage after participating
insights.locator: Last year, Communio churches saw 22,149 people

insights.claim: Churches running Communio ministries report large attendance growth: Mountain View Baptist (Tucson) drew 1,600+ to its ministry with 50%+ Sunday-attendance growth, and St. Helen's (Vero Beach) saw a two-thirds rise in annual weddings and 54% Sunday-attendance growth since 2023.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, James Lynch
insights.so_what: Suggests marriage/family ministry is also a church-growth strategy, aligning institutional self-interest with family revival.
insights.quote: it has seen Sunday attendance grow by 54% since 2023
insights.locator: Out in Vero Beach, Florida, St. Helen’s Church

insights.claim: Almost two-thirds of single young adults are not dating seriously — a 'dating market recession' driven by lack of confidence, no promising partners, or prior negative experiences.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, James Lynch
insights.so_what: Quantifies the upstream pipeline collapse feeding marriage decline — the choke point any family-formation strategy must address.
insights.quote: almost two-thirds of single young adults are not dating seriously
insights.locator: Right now, almost two-thirds of single young adults

insights.claim: Parental religious attendance/prayer, a warm parent-child relationship, and a stable marriage are the three most powerful engines of faith transmission — more than any youth group, Sunday school, or church camp combined, per a Communio/IFS report; kids with a 'very good' relationship with both parents were 97% more likely to believe in God as adults.
insights.speaker: Brad Wilcox, James Lynch
insights.so_what: Locates faith retention in the home, not programs — redirecting church resources toward equipping parents.
insights.quote: more powerful than any youth group, Sunday school program or church camp combined
insights.locator: A new report from Communio and the Institute for Family Studies
